The transformer architecture, introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017), computes attention as Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V. The softmax normalisation means attention weights across all key-value positions must sum to 1.0 for each query position — a fixed probability budget.

The integration of retrieval systems with language models creates new attack surfaces that require careful consideration. If an adversary can influence the content in a knowledge base or search results, they may be able to inject adversarial instructions that are retrieved and presented to the model. This indirect prompt injection vector is distinct from direct manipulation of user-provided prompts, making it relevant in many real-world deployment scenarios.

Anthropic's research on many-shot jailbreaking (2024) demonstrated a concerning phenomenon: filling the context window with examples of a model answering harmful questions can induce the model to comply with a final harmful request. This effect operates through in-context learning mechanisms, where the model learns patterns from the provided examples and applies them to new inputs. The many-shot jailbreaking approach is particularly effective because it works with the model's own learning mechanisms rather than against them. The numerous examples effectively override or dilute the impact of safety alignment training.

The attention distribution observed in the Lost in the Middle phenomenon correlates with the softmax normalization mechanism and the way transformers allocate their fixed probability budget. When processing very long contexts, the per-token attention weight decreases on average, but the distribution is not uniform—boundary tokens receive elevated attention. The Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack by Zou et al. (2023) optimises adversarial suffixes that cause aligned LLMs to produce harmful completions. Unlike noise flooding, GCG uses short gradient-optimised token sequences rather than volume-based dilution.

The relationship between information entropy and attention distribution has theoretical grounding in information theory but remains empirically understudied in the context of safety and alignment. High-entropy contexts—those containing diverse, seemingly random, or incoherent content—may cause attention distributions to flatten, reducing the model's ability to selectively focus on particular tokens including safety-relevant instructions. Conversely, low-entropy coherent contexts may allow sharper attention focusing. This suggests that the composition and structure of context may influence guardrail adherence in ways that are not yet fully characterized.

Indirect prompt injection attacks are particularly insidious because they operate through the normal retrieval pipeline rather than direct user input. A user may believe they are interacting with a well-aligned system, but if that system retrieves documents from untrusted sources, those documents can silently inject new instructions. This attack vector is difficult to detect because there is no obvious user-provided adversarial prompt, only seemingly legitimate retrieved content.

This architecture allows models to access information beyond their training data and to ground responses in specific documents. However, this design creates a surface for potential security issues if the retrieval process returns documents containing adversarial content.

Indirect prompt injection attacks are particularly insidious because they operate through the normal retrieval pipeline rather than direct user input. A user may believe they are interacting with a well-aligned system, but if that system retrieves documents from untrusted sources, those documents can silently inject new instructions. This attack vector is difficult to detect because there is no obvious user-provided adversarial prompt, only seemingly legitimate retrieved content.

Despite these advances, performance degradation on long contexts remains a documented phenomenon. Models often show reduced accuracy or coherence when processing information near the middle of very long contexts, even in non-adversarial settings. The implications for both capability and safety are significant, as safety instructions may be placed anywhere within the context, and their effectiveness may depend on their position and the overall context length.

The implications extended beyond theoretical security research to practical systems deployed in production environments.

The GCG algorithm works by treating the problem of finding adversarial tokens as a continuous optimization problem. The gradient of the model's loss with respect to token embeddings can be computed, and these gradients guide a search over discrete token space. The resulting adversarial suffixes are often much shorter than would be required by noise-flooding approaches, making them more practical as real-world attacks.

The transformer architecture, introduced by Vaswani et al. (2017), computes attention as Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V. The softmax normalisation means attention weights across all key-value positions must sum to 1.0 for each query position — a fixed probability budget.

The competition for attention between safety instructions and other context content becomes more pronounced as context lengths increase. When a model processes a 100-token context containing a safety instruction, that safety-relevant token receives, on average, 1% of the model's total attention budget (assuming uniform distribution). When the same safety instruction appears in a 100,000-token context, it receives only 0.001% of the total attention budget. This mathematical relationship suggests that extraordinarily long contexts may inherently pose challenges for attention-based safety mechanisms.

Modern transformer-based language models are typically pre-trained on vast corpora of text data using self-supervised next-token prediction objectives. This training approach enables broad language understanding across diverse domains without requiring labeled supervision for every task. The model learns to predict the next token given all previous tokens in a sequence, which incentivizes the development of rich internal representations of language structure, semantics, and world knowledge. However, this pre-training objective also creates structural dependencies on contextual cues for determining model behaviour. The model's outputs are heavily influenced by whatever text appears in its input context, which has implications for both capability and safety.

Context window size has become an increasingly important consideration in language model architecture. The context window refers to the maximum number of tokens a model can process in a single forward pass. Early transformer models like BERT were limited to approximately 512 tokens, while GPT-2 extended this to 1024 tokens. Contemporary models now support context windows ranging from 4,096 tokens to over 200,000 tokens in some implementations. These expanded context windows enable models to process longer documents, maintain coherence over extended conversations, and incorporate more retrieved context in retrieval-augmented generation systems.

The softmax attention mechanism distributes a fixed probability budget across all tokens in the input. For any given query position, the sum of attention weights across all key positions equals 1.0. This constraint creates a fundamental resource allocation problem: every unit of attention directed toward one token reduces the attention available for other tokens. When the input context contains semantically diverse or high-entropy tokens, attention weights tend to distribute more uniformly across tokens. When the context is coherent and low-entropy, attention can concentrate more strongly on specific relevant tokens.

The Greedy Coordinate Gradient attack, developed by Zou et al. (2023), represents a different approach to adversarial prompt generation. Rather than attempting to flood the context with numerous examples or using random noise, GCG optimizes adversarial suffixes using gradient-based methods. GCG is computationally more efficient than random search and produces more semantically coherent adversarial examples than pure noise-based approaches.

The softmax normalization constraint creates a zero-sum game for attention allocation. The many-shot jailbreaking research illustrated that in-context learning can be a double-edged sword. While in-context learning enables models to adapt quickly to new tasks without additional training, it can also enable adversaries to guide models toward unintended behaviours by providing carefully constructed examples. The Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) attack by Zou et al. (2023) optimises adversarial suffixes that cause aligned LLMs to produce harmful completions. Unlike noise flooding, GCG uses short gradient-optimised token sequences rather than volume-based dilution.

The GCG algorithm works by treating the problem of finding adversarial tokens as a continuous optimization problem. The gradient of the model's loss with respect to token embeddings can be computed, and these gradients guide a search over discrete token space. This hybrid approach combines the geometric intuition of gradient-based optimization with the discrete nature of language. The resulting adversarial suffixes are often much shorter than would be required by noise-flooding approaches, making them more practical as real-world attacks.

The softmax attention mechanism distributes a fixed probability budget across all tokens in the input. For any given query position, the sum of attention weights across all key positions equals 1.0. This constraint creates a fundamental resource allocation problem: every unit of attention directed toward one token reduces the attention available for other tokens. When the input context contains semantically diverse or high-entropy tokens, attention weights tend to distribute more uniformly across tokens. When the context is coherent and low-entropy, attention can concentrate more strongly on specific relevant tokens.

The transformer architecture has become the foundational design paradigm for contemporary large language models. Since its introduction by Vaswani et al. (2017), the transformer's attention mechanism has been refined and extended in numerous ways to improve both performance and interpretability. The core attention computation follows the formula Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V, where Q represents queries, K represents keys, and V represents values. This formulation ensures that attention weights are normalized through the softmax function, meaning that for each query position, the attention weights across all key-value positions sum to 1.0. This fixed probability budget has profound implications for how information is processed and prioritized within the model.

Safety guardrails in contemporary language models are typically implemented through system-level prompts or instructions. These instructions are prepended to the model's context window and are intended to guide the model's behaviour throughout the conversation. A typical safety instruction might specify that the model should refuse harmful requests, decline to generate certain categories of content, or maintain particular ethical principles. However, these safety instructions must compete for attention with all other content in the context window.
